coredata.dc:title: High performance carbon/silica co-decorated TiO2 nanotubes for visible-light driven water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xu
Liang, Huagen
Wu, Liangpeng
Zhang, Jiafeng
Huang, Yanqin
Li, Xinjun
coredata.dc:description: A novel carbon/silica co-decorated TiO2 nanotubes (TNT) photocatalyst (CS@TNT) was prepared for visible light driven water splitting by a facile two-steps process of amino-propyl-triethoxy silane modification and calcination. Structure characterizations (XRD, STEM, XPS, UV–vis spectra and PL spectra) revealed that the silica and carbon dots were homogeneously dispersed on the surfaces of TNT. The introduced silica resists crystallization of TNT, and keeps the tubular morphology intact and high surface area; the co-existence of carbon dots increases the visible light absorbance capacity and promotes the electron-hole separation efficiency. The CS@TNT preserves intact tubular morphology during calcination (500°C), and a high activity of 0.096mmol/h is achieved, which was 1.7 times higher than that of actual catalyst made of gold nanoparticles loading on TNT.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 nanotube
Silane modification
Calcination temperature
Water splitting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation route of carbon/silica co-modified TNT (CS@TNT).
TG and DSC curves of as-prepared KH570@TNT.
N2 isothermal adsorption-desorption curves (a) and pore size distribution plots (b) for various samples.
HRTEM images of TNT, S@TNT and CS@TNT.
STEM images and elements Mapping of CS@TNT.
C1s, O 2p, Si 2p and Ti 2p XPS spectra for various samples.
UV–vis absorbtion spectra (a) and transformed diffuse reflectance spectra (b) of samples.
Hydrogen evolution towards water splitting for various catalysts (a) and CS@TNT calcinated at various temperatures (b).
Mesoporous parameters of samples calcinated at 500°C.
XPS analysis of fresh catalysts: binding energy and atomic ratio.

head.ce:title: High performance carbon/silica co-decorated TiO2 nanotubes for visible-light driven water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Renewable Energy, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New and Renewable Energy Research and Development, Guangzhou Institute of Energy Conversion, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New and Renewable Energy Research and Development

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Low Carbon Energy Institute, School of Chemical Engineering & Technology, China University of Mining and Technology, Xuzhou, 221116, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China University of Mining and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using amino-propyl-triethoxy silane (KH570) as silane couplers, a novel carbon/silica dots co-decorated TiO2 nanotube (CS@TNT) is prepared through silane modification and thermal calcination approach. The CS@TNT retains its tubular morphology at as high as 500°C, and presents a high activity (0.096mmol/h) towards photocatalytic water splitting.

A novel carbon/silica co-decorated TiO2 nanotubes (TNT) photocatalyst (CS@TNT) was prepared for visible light driven water splitting by a facile two-steps process of amino-propyl-triethoxy silane modification and calcination. Structure characterizations (XRD, STEM, XPS, UV–vis spectra and PL spectra) revealed that the silica and carbon dots were homogeneously dispersed on the surfaces of TNT. The introduced silica resists crystallization of TNT, and keeps the tubular morphology intact and high surface area; the co-existence of carbon dots increases the visible light absorbance capacity and promotes the electron-hole separation efficiency. The CS@TNT preserves intact tubular morphology during calcination (500°C), and a high activity of 0.096mmol/h is achieved, which was 1.7 times higher than that of actual catalyst made of gold nanoparticles loading on TNT.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic water splitting is a promising technology to generate H2 directly from solar energy since the pioneer work by the Honda and Fujishima [1]. So far, various kinds of materials, such as TiO2 [2–4], ZnO [5,6], AgPO3 [7], SiC [8], C3N4 [9] and etc, have been developed to for water splitting.

Among those well examined semiconductor materials, TiO2 nanotube (TNT) is a cost-effective and eco-friendly candidate, and has attracted an enormous amount of interest since its discovery [10–13]. The TNT holds tubular cavity and abundant −OH groups attaching to the TNT walls. The former can contribute to its high surface area; and the latter could facilitate its modification with various other elements (C, N, S, P, Pt, Au and etc.) [14–17]. However, the large band gap of pristine TNT (3.2eV) implies that this material can only be activated using an irradiation with photons in the UV region (λ ≤387 nm). In addition, another challenging obstacle limitting the application of pristine TNT is the fact that the tubular structure is apt to collapse undergoing calcination (T> 400°C) or hydrothermal treatment. Therefore, to exert the promosing features of TNT, there is a need for great effort to explore a feasible strategy to improve the TNT’s visible-light response capability as well as tubular structure stability.

There exists kinds of way to obtain activity in the visible region for the TiO2 based materials, such as dye sensitization [18], loading transition metal, doping non-metal ions and so on [19–26]. Of these reported protocols, carbon doping brings considerable potential advantages since Kisch and co-workers [21] for the first time reported the carbon-modified TiO2 via hydrothermal method. Carbon can increase the adsorption capability in the visible light region, thus increase the life-span of photo-excited electron-hole pairs [26–28]. The deposited or doped carbon can act as electron reservoir to trap photo-excited electrons from semi-conductors. However, the common protocols (e.g. hydrothermal or high temperature calcination) to prepare carbon-doping TiO2 is not adapted for the TNT, because the tubular structure would collapse during hydrothermal or calcination process as we mentioned above.

Recently, we have developed a silica modified TNT [29] that can subject to the calcination as high as 500°C without collapse of tubular structure. Inspiringly, we find that various elements (such as carbon or other inorganic elements) can be introduced spontaneously into the TNT through silane modification by adjusting the selected modifiers and thermal treatment manner (in air or H2/N2 atmosphere). Based on this finding, herein we attempt to develop a novel and facile route to obtain a carbon/silica co-decorated TNT (CS@TNT) using two steps process of amino-propyl-triethoxy silane (KH570) modification and calcination. This novel CS@TNT catalyst was applied for visible light driven water splitting, and the carbon/silica co-modification effect was investigated in detail.

TiO2 nanotubes (TNT) were synthesized by a route described in reference [10]. As-received TiO2 (Hualisen Corp., China) was mixed with 100mL concentrated NaOH solution (10M) and placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave under mild stirring at 140°C for 24h, then the resultant white precipitate was separated from the mixture and washed with deionized water several times until the conductivity of the solution remained below 100μscm−1.

Amino-propyl-triethoxy silane (KH570) was used as silane couplers (see Scheme 1 for their molecular structures). Modification of TNT was conducted as follows: in a mass ratio of 2:1, pristine TNT and KH570 were added to a water–ethanol mixed solution under vigorous stirring at 50°C for 5h. The resulting white precipitate was filtered, washed with ethanol to eliminate the extra KH570, and dried at 60°C for overnight. The as-prepared sample was denoted as KH570@TNT.

The KH570@TNT was calcinated in the H2 atmosphere in the oven at various temperatures (from 200 to 700°C) for 3h to transform the silane into carbon and silica deposited on TNT. We denoted the carbon/silica co-decorated TNT as CS@TNT. For reference, a pure silica decorated TNT (S@TNT) sample was prepared using the same route but calcination in the air atmosphere at 500°C.

Thermo-gravimetric (TG) and differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) analysis was carried out in N2, in a STA409C/PC unit (NETZSCH, German), at a heating rate of 10°C/min, from room temperature to 1000°C. The amount of sample used was 15mg. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were measured with a Tristar 3010 isothermal nitrogen sorption analyzer (Micromeritics, USA) using a continuous adsorption procedure. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a D/Max-IIIA X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) using Cu Kα radiation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out on a JEM-2010 microscope (JEOL, Japan) using an accelerating voltage of 200kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with an AXIS Ultra DLD (Kratos, Britain) to examine the catalysts’ electronic properties. Diffuse reflectance UV–vis (DRUV–vis) spectra were recorded with a Lambda 750 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA), equipped with an integrating sphere where the baseline was recorded using a bare titania powders. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of these powders were obtained on a Spectro MAXx (SPECTRO, German) with a laser excitation of 325nm.

The photocatalytic splitting of water to generate hydrogen was carried out using the lab-made device. In a typical reaction [19], 10mg of photocatalysts were added to 100mL of methanol-deionized water mixed solution (V MeOH /V H2O =1:10) in the Pyrex reactor. The reactor was irradiated using a Xe lamp (λ>400nm) after the residual air in the reactor was confirmed to be negligible. The intensity of incident visible light (λ>400nm) was 0.1W/cm2, which was projected onto the reactor-side surface of 5cm2. The reaction products were analyzed by GC. Blank reactions were conducted without photocatalyst in the presence of light and with photocatalyst in the dark. In both cases, no production of hydrogen was observed. The apparent quantum efficiency (QE) was determined under the similar photocatalytic reaction conditions. The QE was measured and calculated according to Eq. following:
QE
( % )
=
n u m b e r
o f
e v o l v e d
H
2
m o l e c u l e s × 2
n u m b e r
o f
i n c i d e n t
p h o t o n s
× 100

Firstly, the as-prepared KH570@TNT was characterized by the TG and DSC, as shown in Fig. 1 . A mainly weigh loss (12%) in temperature range from room temperature to 500°C is observed in the TG curve. In the DSC curves, an endothermic peak at 100°C and an exothermic peak at 286°C can be seen, which are due to the evaporation of the physically adsorbed water and decomposition of the introduced KH570, respectively. Higher than 500°C, negligible mass lost can be seen, revealing the constant composition of KH570@TNT after calcination at 500°C.

Fig. 2 shows the XRD of various samples calcinated at 500°C. The pure TNT exhibits a typical anatase TiO2 structure with space group I41/amd (JCPDS card No. 21-1272) [30]. Compared with the pure TNT, the diffraction intensity of the CS@TNT and S@TNT is relatively weak, especially for S@TNT. Applying the Scherrer equation to the (101) peak of each sample, the particle crystallite sizes of TNT, CS@TNT and S@TNT were estimated to be 14.5, 10.3 and 8.2nm, respectively. Okada and et al. [31] reported a similar result that the crystallinity of anatase decreased and the crystallization temperature increased upon the addition of silica. We attributed this to the deposition of silica, revealing that silica modification obstructs the crystallization of the TNT support.

Fig. 3 shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm curves for the samples. All of the samples show a typical H3 hysteresis loop according to BDDT classification [32], indicating the presence of mesopores (2–50nm). The observed hysteresis loops approach P/P0
=1, suggesting the presence of macropores (>50nm) [33]. The corresponding pore size distributions of the samples confirm the two type pores: the smaller one (<10nm) may correspond to the pores inside the nanotubes, while the larger one (10–100nm) can be attributed to the aggregation of the nanotubes. Textural parameters derived from the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm data are summarized in Table 1 . The CS@TNT and S@TNT show high surface area of 237 and 212m2/g, respectively, which are comparable with the as-prepared TNT (220m2/g); while the calcinated TNT displays a sharp decreased surface area of 81m2/g. This suggests that the silica decoration can retain the high surface area of samples undergoing calcination, which could provide more of active sites accessible to the reactants. The surface area shrink of calcinated TNT can be associated with the collapse of tubular cavity upon calcination, which is verified by the following TEM observation.

Fig. 4 shows the HRTEM images of corresponding samples. The intact tubular cavity is seen clearly for the as-prepared TNT, S@TNT and CS@TNT. While the sample TNT calcinated at 500°C displays short nano-rods, suggesting the collapse of tubular structure. This is responsible for the sharp decrease of surface area. In documents elsewhere [34,35], several unmodified TNT could transform into nano-rod or collapse to nano-particles when calcinated at 500°C. It demonstrates well the crucial role of silica modification to maintain its tubular morphology. The magnified images of CS@TNT indicates clearly the inner pore of 5nm, and the crystalline lattice of 0.305nm, assigning to the (101) plane of anatase. The presence of silica and carbon species cannot be seen from the present HRTEM observation, suggesting the homogeneous dispersion of carbon and silica dots, or trace amount that beyond the detection limit of TEM.

To characterize more clearly the distribution of carbon and silica dots on TNT, we conducted the elements mapping for CS@TNT, seeing Fig. 5 . It verifies the co-existence of silica and carbon after calcination; the silica and carbon dots dispersed uniformly on the TNT. In addition, the elements mapping gives the weight ratio of C and Si, 8.4% and 1.7%, respectively.

The XPS spectra of Si 2p showed a single peak located at binding energies of 102±0.4eV, which is assigned to the Si-O bond. The atomic ratio of Si/Ti is kept constant (seeing Table 2) for both S@TNT and CS@TNT, indicating the inertness of silica. The XPS spectra of Ti 2p at 458.9eV are attributed to the Ti-O bonding energy. The integral peak of O1s decomposes into two components, oxygen species with binding energy of 530.0 and 532.0eV, correspond to the oxygen in the composition of the three dimensional titanium oxide (TiO) [38], and oxygen in the composition of the OH group or water [39]. The binding energies of the Ti and O elements shift clearly after decoration of C and Si, indicating charge transfer occurred between the components after decoration of C and Si.

Fig. 7 a shows the UV–vis spectra of catalysts. As expected, the as-prepared pristine TNT (not shown for brevity) and S@TNT show the characteristic spectrum of pristine TiO2 with its fundamental absorption sharp edge rising at 400nm, indicating the innerness of visible light (λ>400nm) activity. It is clearly that the absorption edge of CS@TNT shifts to the visible-light region, meanwhile a long-tail absorption in the visible-light region increases as well. Correspondingly, the band gaps of the compounds were estimated (seeing Fig. 7b) from diffuse reflectance spectra following a method based on Tauc equation and described elsewhere [40]. For the pristine TNT, and S@TNT, the band gap is found at 3.20eV. The CS@TNT shows a narrowed band gap of 2.33eV. The narrower band-gap energies were caused by either the doped carbons at interstitial positions in the TiO2 lattice or the substitution of carbon with oxygen induced several localized occupied states in the gap [18,30,31]. This can promote the production of photo-excited electrons-hole pairs.

Fig. 8 shows the Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of all samples. The samples are excited at 325nm. The highest PL emission intensity is presented in the S@TNT, followed by the TNT, suggesting that the recombination of electron-hole pairs is the severe for the two samples. Obviously, the PL intensity decreases sharply for the CS@TNT. This demonstrates that the decoration of carbon dots on the TNT could accelerate the electron-hole pair separation, improving the photocatalytic activity.

The performance towards photocatalytic H2 production of the catalysts was evaluated and compared under visible (λ>400nm) light irradiation. Fig. 9 a shows the bar chart of H2 production per hour on various samples. Using the pristine TNT (Fig. 9a) and Degussa P25 (not shown for brevity) as catalyst, negligible hydrogen is produced for both samples, indicating the inertness of pure TNT and P25 in the given reaction conditions. The single silica modified TNT (S@TNT) shows slight photocatalytic activity as well under the given reaction parameters, implying the silica modification have little effect on the photocatalytic activity. Noticeably, the CS@TNT produces 0.096mmol per hour of H2, which is nearly 1.7 times higher than that of our previously reported Au/TNT [41]. An apparent quantum efficiency of ca. 3.5% is achieved for the CS@TNT, comparable with the values reported by literatures reported elsewhere [42,43]. The CS@TNT presents 7% decline of activity at the third times recycle-utilization, implying its good stability. This enhancement seems to results from the carbon modification, instead of silica. As justified in the UV–vis and PL spectra, the deposited carbon dots increases the visible light absorbance capacity and promotes the electron-hole separation efficiency. In addition, the high surface area of CS@TNT can provide more of active site that accessible to the reactants.

Illustrations of the effect of calcination temperature on the photocatalytic activity of CS@TNT are shown in Fig. 1b. A volcano curve is obtained for the CS@TNT as temperature evolution, with the optimized temperature at 500°C. It verifies the importance of calcination temperature for the water splitting performance. Calcination facilitates the deposition and doping of carbon dots on the TNT, and improves the crystalline degree. Further elevating the temperature (>500°C) causes the collapse of tubular structure and shrink of surface area, which may be an important reason for the activity decline.

A novel carbon and silica co-decorated TNT was prepared through silane modification and thermal calcination approach. The physicochemical properties of CS@TNT were characterized in detail by XRD, N2 sorption, TEM, XPS, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy and Photoluminescence spectra. The results indicate that the carbon and silica dots are well-dispersed on the TNT, and the CS@TNT can preserve its tubular structure and high surface area as high as 500°C of calcination. Applied for photocatalytic water splitting, the CS@TNT presents enhanced hydrogen evolution rate of 0.096mmol/h. The enhancement of photocatalytic activity could be attributed to the silica and carbon co-modification effect: the introduced silica resists crystallization of TNT, and keeps the tubular morphology intact as well as high surface area during calcination; while the co-existence of carbon dots increases the visible light absorbance capacity and promotes the electron-hole separation efficiency.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Scientific Foundation of China (project No. 21303210, No. 51661145022).
